artifacts.content:
Hi Kim,

Writing this from the couch after finally getting Evie down. She insisted on sleeping in her unicorn sneakers again, so I picked my battles and let it go.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how we got here with Riverbend. I told one of our new volunteers the other day that a big part of why I am so stubborn about keeping every lab, field trip, and summer camp 100% free — including bus passes and pizza — goes back to that mess in 2019. I can still picture you calling me from Chicago on March 3, crying because Mom and Dad had just gotten that shutoff notice in the mail at the Dayton apartment. Three months behind on rent and utilities while they were quietly helping with my UC grad school loans and Michael’s Riverside Methodist ER bill from March 2018. We were all working full-time and still hanging on by a thread.

Then a couple years later, May 2021, I spent three Saturdays in a row (the 8th, 15th, and 22nd) driving them from Dayton to the IRS Taxpayer Assistance Center downtown over that $7,400 back-tax issue from the 2017–2018 returns your favorite “cash-only preparer” on West Third Street messed up. Sitting there in that fluorescent waiting room translating IRS jargon while Dad signed the installment agreement is burned into my brain.

All of that is why I’m so fixated on FAFSA nights, scholarship help, and not charging families a dime. No kid should miss robotics because the lights might get turned off.

Anyway, how’s your week on the unit? Call this weekend if you’re not on nights.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: Why I keep pushing so hard at Riverbend

artifacts.content:
Hey Michael,

I needed to get this all out of my head before I forget the details.

First, Jason Miller. After the February 10, 2025 Columbus Police wellness check at the Hilltop Community Center robotics lab, I’m still second‑guessing how we handled it. We pulled him quietly from the club that night, but I’m not sure how much to put in his file or whether we owe the parents of the 14 kids who were there some sort of communication. I talked with Melissa Harper after the board meeting — she’s been great, but even she says we need real HR/legal guidance this spring (March–June 2025) to update our volunteer screening and incident‑response policies so they’re trauma‑informed and don’t stigmatize him.

Then there’s the money mess. When I sat down with Alan Rodriguez on January 8, 2025 for that informal review of our FY2023–24 books, we found almost $4,800 in double mileage and supply reimbursements Sarah L. ran through the West Town Street site over about six months. I feel sick even typing that. Alan thinks we can fix it, but we’ll need pro bono accounting help in May to clean everything up and make sure it’s disclosed correctly in the audit/990 so it looks like an internal control failure we’re addressing, not a cover‑up.

Meanwhile, Evie has a pajama day on Friday and I still haven’t washed her favorite llama set, and I promised you I’d actually go to bed before midnight at least twice this week.

Can we do a budget/mental health check date night this weekend? Maybe just tacos and a walk along the Scioto.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: Brain dump: Jason, books, and tacos

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – home, work, and kids

I keep circling back to October 14, 2020. Standing outside Avondale Elementary with my coffee, and Ms. Harper quietly asking if everything was okay at home because Evie had told her, “Daddy sleeps on the couch now.” All because of that loud argument in the living room at 1243 West Town Street, Apartment 2B about my late nights at Riverbend and Michael’s anxiety flare‑ups. He’d just started seeing Dr. Patel at OSU Harding Hospital, and I was glued to my laptop every evening. No wonder Evie noticed.

That conversation is what pushed me to double down on trauma‑informed training for staff. Kids bring all of that chaos into our after‑school STEM labs, just like Evie brought ours into kindergarten.

Fast‑forward to October 3, 2024 on the COTA #10 route, riding back from the West Town Street lab with the Avondale 8th graders, when Mrs. Hernandez’s daughter had that severe asthma attack. The driver refused to wait while I used her inhaler, and Mrs. Hernandez understandably filed a complaint with COTA and Columbus City Schools transportation. I keep replaying it and wondering how many other kids are one bus ride away from disaster.

So: home tension, school worries, transportation gaps — it’s all the same ecosystem. Need to remember this when I’m tempted to reduce Riverbend to “just” coding and robots.

Action items for me:
- Keep therapy on the table for myself.
- Push for updated MOUs with COTA/district for medically fragile students.
- Protect at least two evenings a week as no‑work time at home.

Event: Dayton trip – Dad neurology follow‑up + assisted‑living tours

Date: Thursday, 2025-07-10
Time: 11:00 a.m. – 9:00 p.m. (block full day)
Location: Miami Valley Hospital + Dayton assisted‑living facilities
Attendees: Me, Dad (Thomas Nguyen), Mom (Lan Pham Nguyen)

Notes:
- Leave Columbus by 11:00 a.m. from 1243 West Town Street, Apartment 2B.
- Neurology follow‑up at Miami Valley Hospital in Dayton re: Dad’s early‑stage Parkinson’s diagnosis from November 2024. Bring list of meds + notes on his hand tremors and balance issues.
- After appointment, tour at least two assisted‑living options nearby with Mom and Dad. Ask about Vietnamese meals, transportation to the temple, and payment plans.
- I’ve already missed three Riverbend evening programs this school year for neurology appointments; need to talk with staff about cross‑training and backup leadership for nights when I’m in Dayton.
- Summer 2025 goal: work with board on succession/contingency plan in case my caregiving responsibilities ramp up and I have to reduce hours as Executive Director.
- Call Kim in Chicago on drive back so she feels looped in on decisions.
- Remember to pack snacks, water, and Dad’s sweater (waiting rooms are always freezing).
- Ask Michael to handle Evie pickup from Avondale aftercare and bedtime routine.

metadata.title: Drive to Dayton – Dad neuro f/u + assisted‑living tours

artifacts.content:
Hi Jamal,

Flagging a couple of things that have been weighing on me as we think about Riverbend’s next phase.

First, the transportation situation. Since that October 3, 2024 incident on the COTA #10 bus — when one of our 8th graders from Avondale had a severe asthma attack on the ride back from the West Town Street lab and the driver refused to wait while I administered her inhaler — I’ve been uneasy. Her mom, Mrs. Hernandez, filed complaints with both COTA and the Columbus City Schools transportation office. I’d really like some pro bono policy/advocacy help to renegotiate our MOU with COTA and the district so medically fragile students can still access evening programs safely.

Second, communications. On April 14, 2024, a parent, Angela Brooks, posted on Facebook about our Hilltop Community Center program, accusing us of “tracking Black kids into tech instead of fixing their reading.” She referenced my comment at the March 28 Family STEM Night where I used Evie’s kindergarten literacy struggles at Avondale as an example of how culturally relevant STEM can boost confidence. I meant it as a vulnerable, personal story, but it clearly landed badly with her and some others; the post has been shared in at least two local parent groups.

I’m worried both issues could erode trust with families and school partners if we don’t handle them thoughtfully. Would you be open to helping me identify folks who might donate a few hours of crisis‑comms and policy support? I want to own my missteps, listen well, and still protect the integrity of the work.

Happy to talk more at our next check‑in.

Best,
Danielle

Hi Melissa,

Thanks again for taking my panicked call after the board meeting. I wanted to summarize everything while it’s fresh.

On February 10, 2025, during our robotics club at the Hilltop Community Center, Columbus Police officers did a wellness check on Jason Miller after his off‑site mental health crisis. We pulled him from the lab quietly that night and have not scheduled him since. I know we need to document the incident clearly in his volunteer file, but I’m torn about how much detail is appropriate and whether we have any obligation to notify the parents of the 14 students who were present. I want our response to be trauma‑informed and supportive, not punitive or stigmatizing.

At the same time, I’m increasingly aware that I’m personally too enmeshed in everything. I keep flashing back to Fall 2020, when Evie’s kindergarten teacher at Avondale, Ms. Harper, pulled me aside on October 14 because Evie had told her, “Daddy sleeps on the couch now,” after that awful argument Michael and I had in our living room at 1243 West Town Street about my late nights at Riverbend and his anxiety treatment with Dr. Patel at OSU Harding Hospital. That was a wake‑up call about how much the job was bleeding into home.

Now we’ve got Jason’s situation, the transportation complaints, and the usual funding scramble, and I can feel the same patterns creeping back in.

Would you be willing to review a draft of updated volunteer screening/incident‑response policies and tell me where we need outside HR counsel? I’d also welcome any advice on boundaries so I’m not the default crisis manager for every situation.

With gratitude,
Danielle

Running list – why Riverbend does more than “fun STEM”

1. Family financial fragility
- March 3, 2019: Kim calling from Chicago in tears because Mom and Dad got the shutoff notice at their Dayton apartment. Three months behind on rent and utilities after helping with my grad school loans and Michael’s March 2018 ER bill at Riverside Methodist Hospital.
- That feeling of being one bill away from losing everything is why we cover transportation, meals, and never charge for camps.

2. Navigating intimidating systems
- May 8, 15, 22, 2021: three Saturdays driving Mom and Dad from Dayton to the IRS Taxpayer Assistance Center downtown over the $7,400 back‑tax issue from their 2017–2018 returns (thanks to the cash‑only preparer on West Third Street). Translating jargon, watching Dad sign the installment agreement.
- Direct line to our FAFSA/college‑access nights and one‑on‑one support.

3. Court‑involved youth
- Spring 2019: six Thursday afternoons outside Courtroom 4B at Franklin County Juvenile Court with Aisha while Malik (pseudonym) went before Judge + probation officer Mr. Reynolds. He questioned whether Malik’s weekly attendance at High School Tech Pathways at Hilltop Community Center “really counted” as a constructive activity.
- That’s when we started drafting court‑friendly attendance documentation and talking explicitly about supporting youth whose school/legal records don’t show their potential.

Remember: these are my internal anchors, not stories to center publicly. Use them to stay grounded when the work feels like grant cycles and spreadsheets instead of real kids and families.
